queue.title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute — Ryan Lopopolo, OpenAI
queue.summary: Ryan Lopopolo from OpenAI explores the paradigm shift where AI agents handle code implementation. He argues that human engineers must evolve from writing code to steering systems, designing guardrails, and managing AI agent teams. Key takeaway: human attention is now the most scarce resource in engineering. Beneficial for software engineers and tech leaders adapting to AI-assisted development.

queue.title: How the Iran War Spiked Oil Prices
queue.summary: Examines the geopolitical and economic consequences of shutting down the Strait of Hormuz. While the Strait handles 20% of the world's oil, global production only fell by 8% due to strategic bypass pipelines. Discusses demand elasticity to explain why even small supply drops cause massive price spikes. Beneficial for students of economics, geopolitics, and international relations.

queue.title: Re:ZERO Season 3 Full Recap | Everything You Need To Know For Season 4
queue.summary: This video provides a detailed summary of the Watergate City of Priestella arc from Re:Zero, covering the Emilia camp's diplomatic mission and the ensuing battle against the Witch Cult. It highlights major plot points, such as Subaru's limited use of Return by Death and his evolution into a hero for the masses, while resolving personal subplots for characters like Wilhelm and Garfield. Fans of the series looking for a concise refresher on character growth and arc outcomes before continuing the story will find this summary particularly beneficial.

queue.title: Stanford CS229 I Machine Learning I Building Large Language Models (LLMs)
queue.summary: This video features Yann Dubois from Stanford Engineering discussing the core components involved in building Large Language Models (LLMs). He breaks down the five key factors of LLM development—architecture, training algorithms, data, evaluation, and systems—while emphasizing the practical importance of data and evaluation in industry. The lecture covers pretraining and post-training phases, explaining how autoregressive models use tokenization and cross-entropy loss to predict text sequences. Ideal for CS students or researchers interested in the engineering behind tools like ChatGPT and Gemini.

queue.title: Code Mode: Let the Code do the Talking - Sunil Pai, Cloudflare
queue.summary: Sunil Pai from Cloudflare introduces 'Code Mode,' where AI models generate code like JavaScript to interact with systems instead of traditional JSON-based tool calling. This approach reduces token usage by 99.9% and enables multiple API interactions in a single round trip. By leveraging the model's ability to compose logic through loops and state management, Code Mode provides a faster and more reliable interface for complex tasks.

queue.title: Gemma, DeepMind's Family of Open Models — Omar Sanseviero, Google DeepMind
queue.summary: Overview of Gemma 4, the latest family of open-source AI models from Google DeepMind. Sizes range from 2B to 32B parameters, enabling high performance on consumer GPUs and mobile devices. Key features include on-device offline coding, advanced multimodal understanding across text/images/audio, multilingual support for 140+ languages, and an Apache 2.0 license for community collaboration.

queue.title: ¡NUEVO CLAUDE DESIGN! Un ANTES y DESPUÉS en mi forma de TRABAJAR...
queue.summary: Anthropic has launched Claude Design, a new AI-powered platform for creating websites, documents, and interactive animations through automated HTML programming. Supports custom design systems including color palettes and logos for consistent branding. Simplifies complex workflows like motion graphics with features such as interactive sliders and 3D effects, significantly reducing time for manual design and coding tasks.

queue.title: The Future of MCP — David Soria Parra, Anthropic
queue.summary: Explores the current state and future of the Model Context Protocol (MCP), a standard for connecting AI agents to diverse tools and interfaces. Notes massive growth in MCP adoption and predicts 2026 as the year AI agents move into production. Introduces a 'connectivity stack' of skills, MCP, and CLI/computer use, plus technical improvements like progressive tool discovery and programmatic tool calling.

queue.title: What Is Yann LeCun Cooking? JEPA Explained Simply
queue.summary: Explains JEPA (Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture), an AI concept that focuses on predicting high-dimensional representations in a learned latent space rather than individual pixels or text tokens. By analyzing partial observations of an input, JEPA aims to capture stable semantic structures and physical relationships while discarding irrelevant noise. This approach offers computational efficiency and shows promise for world modeling and planning in fields like robotics.

queue.title: How AI is changing Software Engineering: A Conversation with Gergely Orosz, @pragmaticengineer
queue.summary: Explores 'token maxing,' where engineers at major tech firms like Meta and Microsoft artificially inflate AI token usage to perform well on internal leaderboards tied to performance evaluations. Gergely Orosz explains how this leads employees to prioritize high token counts over genuine productivity, and suggests leadership may be using these metrics to force AI adoption despite conflicting data on AI's actual impact on developer output.

queue.title: Nigel Cheese | Down the Rabbit Hole
queue.summary: Profiles Nigel, who presents unconventional scientific theories including that 1+1=1 and magnets prove cold fusion. Claims to have discovered free limitless energy and a cancer cure through ionized water, countered by science educator Kyle Hill. Highlights Nigel's attempts to validate his identity and belief that his research was suppressed following a Navy raid.

queue.title: Taste & Craft: A Conversation with Tuomas Artman, CTO Linear & Gergely Orosz, @pragmaticengineer
queue.summary: Interview with Tuomas Artman, CTO of Linear, on AI's impact on software development. Cautions against shipping features too quickly using AI agents, emphasizing that quality still requires thoughtful design and discipline. Linear uses AI to automate routine tasks like bug fixing, with ~10% of bugs resolved by AI-generated PRs. Also discusses the difficulty of measuring software quality versus straightforward business metrics.

queue.title: ¡CHATGPT IMAGES 2.0 es ESPECTACULAR! ...PERO tiene UN PROBLEMA
queue.summary: OpenAI has launched a new image generation model, ChatGPT Images 2.0, which significantly advances realism, creativity, and visual intelligence. This model demonstrates exceptional precision in rendering text, complex diagrams, and infographics, while maintaining high consistency when iterating on existing images or reference poses. A key innovation is its integration with deeper reasoning modes, allowing it to research and synthesize complex information before rendering it into high-fidelity visuals. Comparative tests suggest it offers superior control over nuanced details compared to competitors, positioning it as a leading tool for both creative and professional tasks.

queue.title: I forced streamers to make Ice Cream Sandwiches without a recipe | Master Baker Season 5
queue.summary: In the fifth season of 'Master Baker,' host QTCinderella welcomes six amateur bakers to a kitchen competition where they must create dishes without recipes. The first challenge tasks the contestants with making homemade ice cream sandwiches from scratch within a 75-minute timeframe, with judging based on taste, creativity, and execution. As the clock begins, the bakers struggle with basic culinary skills, such as separating eggshells and distinguishing between baking soda and baking powder. The host provides a brief tutorial on the proper technique but notes that some participants may fail to achieve the desired creamy texture by omitting necessary fats from their ice cream base.

queue.title: It Ain't Broke: Why Software Fundamentals Matter More Than Ever — Matt Pocock, AI Hero @mattpocockuk
queue.summary: This video features Matt Pocock's presentation on why traditional software engineering fundamentals remain critical in the age of AI-driven coding. He argues that relying purely on AI to generate code from specifications often leads to 'software entropy,' where code quality degrades with each iteration, making it increasingly expensive to maintain. To counter this, Pocock advocates for techniques like reaching a shared 'design concept' through deep AI questioning and establishing a 'ubiquitous language' based on domain-driven design. Ultimately, he emphasizes that a solid foundation in software design is essential for developers to successfully leverage AI tools while building maintainable systems.

queue.title: The End of Apps — Kitze, Sizzy.co
queue.summary: The speaker describes his decades-long quest for the perfect productivity system, moving from simple text files to a complex 'life OS' called Benji that integrates numerous personal data points. He highlights a shift towards using specialized AI agents, such as openclaw, which operate on locally hosted data to ensure privacy and better contextual awareness. Kitze argues that general-purpose chat interfaces are inadequate for managing life's complexities, advocating instead for specialized agents with distinct permissions and tools. Ultimately, he envisions a future where personal AI assistants have deep control over digital environments, even leading him to switch from iOS to Android for greater system accessibility.

queue.title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor
queue.summary: In this presentation, Eric from Cursor explores the evolution of AI-assisted software development toward a 'software factory' model where AI agents operate with high levels of autonomy. He argues that achieving this requires setting up specific primitives, guardrails like dynamic rules and automated tests, and enablers that give agents access to external context. The shift aims to improve throughput and consistency while allowing human engineers to leverage their creativity more effectively.

queue.title: What Do Models Still Suck At? - Peter Gostev, Arena.ai, BullshitBench
queue.summary: Peter Gostev, AI Capability Lead at Arena, discusses the limitations of current AI models through his 'Bullshit Benchmark,' which tests a model's ability to identify and reject nonsensical prompts. His findings reveal that while some models, particularly those from Anthropic, are improving, many still fail by attempting to rationalize illogical questions. A significant takeaway is that increased reasoning power actually tends to decrease performance in this area, as models spend more tokens trying to solve non-existent problems.

queue.title: Why building eval platforms is hard — Phil Hetzel, Braintrust
queue.summary: Phil Hetzel from Braintrust discusses the evolution and necessity of evaluation (eval) platforms for AI agents, emphasizing that they are critical for moving projects from proof-of-concept to production. He highlights that while many start with simple spreadsheets, robust evals require complex systems that include human annotation, specialized databases, and ongoing observability to handle the high variability of LLMs.

queue.title: Agents on the Canvas in tldraw — Steve Ruiz, tldraw
queue.summary: In this video from the AI Engineer Europe conference, Steve Ruiz, the creator of tldraw, demonstrates the evolution of their online whiteboard and SDK as it integrates with AI. He showcases various experimental features, including "Make Real," which turns sketches into functional prototypes, and an AI collaborator that can draw and iterate on diagrams directly on the canvas. The presentation highlights how tldraw's hackable, React-based infrastructure allows for the creation of sophisticated AI agents that can assist with complex tasks like filling out forms or building interactive diagrams. Developers and AI engineers will benefit from watching this to see how a flexible canvas can be used to build next-generation, collaborative AI experiences.

queue.title: Context Is the New Code — Patrick Debois, Tessl
queue.summary: Patrick Debois explores the evolving role of "context" in AI development, arguing that it is becoming a foundational layer as important as traditional code. He introduces the Context Development Lifecycle (CDLC)—comprising generation, evaluation, distribution, and observation—as a framework for systematically managing how AI models are instructed. The presentation highlights practical techniques for curating context, such as using reusable agent instructions and implementing automated "evals" to ensure AI outputs adhere to specific organizational standards. This talk is highly beneficial for AI engineers and developers seeking to apply disciplined software engineering principles to their work with large language models.

queue.title: I Gave an AI Agent the Keys to My Life (Here's What Happened) — Radek Sienkiewicz (@velvetshark-com)
queue.summary: In this presentation, Radek, a maintainer of Open Interpreter, shares his journey of integrating an AI agent into his daily life until it became essential "life infrastructure." He argues that building such a powerful system should be done incrementally—one permission and workflow at a time—to safely establish trust and define boundaries. A central takeaway is the importance of a vast personal knowledge base, which allows the AI to "connect the dots" across thousands of notes and automate complex, context-aware tasks. This video is highly beneficial for AI engineers and productivity enthusiasts interested in the practical application of autonomous agents for personal digital management.

queue.title: Harness engineering beyond skills: Using sensors to keep your coding agent in check
queue.summary: This video explores "harness engineering," a mental model for optimizing AI coding agents by surrounding them with supportive tools and specific instructions. Birgitta Boeckeler explains how developers can build an "outer harness" using "feedforward" guides (like principles and rules) to improve initial code generation and "feedback" sensors (such as static analysis and logs) to help agents self-correct. The ultimate goal is to increase trust in AI outputs, thereby reducing the burden of human review and promoting more sustainable delivery. This discussion is highly valuable for software engineers and developers looking to integrate AI assistants more effectively into their professional workflows.

queue.title: A Piece of Pi: Embedding The OpenClaw Coding Agent In Your Product — Matthias Luebken, Tavon
queue.summary: This video features Matthias from TAVON.AI presenting A Piece of PI, a talk on embedding the open-source PI coding agent into products. He defines a coding agent as an LLM operating in a loop with access to a runtime and a shell, enabling it to autonomously execute code and perform complex tasks like data analysis or CRM management. A central takeaway is the importance of the make-it-easy-for-coding-agents architectural pattern, which involves designing systems that are simple and accessible for agents to navigate. This presentation is highly beneficial for AI engineers and developers interested in the practical implementation of autonomous agents and agentic workflows.

queue.title: Everything We Got Wrong About Research-Plan-Implement -  Dexter Horthy
queue.summary: This video features Dexter Horthy discussing the lessons learned from deploying AI-driven development workflows across various engineering teams. He emphasizes that while AI can enhance productivity, it often leads to increased rework and requires careful human steering to avoid low-quality outputs. Horthy advocates for engineers to maintain active oversight by avoiding the outsourcing of critical thinking and prioritizing the review of actual code over AI-generated plans. This presentation is highly beneficial for software developers and engineering managers interested in the practical challenges and best practices for integrating AI agents into professional development cycles.

queue.title: Two Roads to Durable Agents: Replay vs. Snapshot — Eric Allam, CEO, Trigger.dev
queue.summary: This video explores the technical evolution and future of durable agents, which are AI systems capable of long-running, meaningful work that remains persistent across errors and code versions. The speaker traces the history of backend architecture from early stateless systems to modern workflow engines, highlighting how traditional replay models struggle with the scale and complexity of agentic sessions. A key takeaway is that true durability requires a dual approach: using append-only logs for an agent's context and conversation history, while utilizing snapshot and restore mechanisms for its execution state, such as memory and file systems. AI engineers and software architects building production-grade agentic workflows would benefit most from watching this talk.

queue.title: Viktor: AI Coworker That Lives in Slack — Fryderyk Wiatrowski
queue.summary: This video introduces Viktor, an AI employee designed to operate directly within Slack to streamline business workflows using over 3,000 integrations. The speaker highlights that Viktor provides a unique horizontal context across all departments, allowing it to act as a company-wide resource rather than a siloed individual. It addresses complex organizational challenges such as shared memory management, departmental privacy, and the evolution from personal agents to team-based assistants. This presentation is particularly beneficial for AI engineers, business leaders, and professionals interested in the practical application of autonomous agents within corporate environments.

queue.title: You can't just one shot it — Mehedi Hassan, Granola
queue.summary: This video features Mehedi Hassan, a Product Engineer at Granola, discussing the challenges of integrating AI features into production environments beyond simple one-shot prompts. He highlights the necessity of molding LLM behavior for specific user roles and the complexities of features like web search. Hassan introduces internal tools developed at Granola, such as custom tracing for agentic loops and a web shell for faster desktop app testing, to facilitate rapid iteration. The core message is that a fast feedback loop is crucial for creating an AI product that feels seamless and intuitive. This presentation is highly beneficial for product engineers and developers looking for practical strategies to refine and improve AI-driven applications.

queue.title: Dark Factory: How OpenClaw Ships Faster Than You Can Read the Diff — Vincent Koc
queue.summary: Vincent Koc discusses the evolution of AI evaluations from static, hand-curated benchmarks to more malleable systems capable of measuring adaptive AI agents. He argues that as AI progresses from prompt engineering to intent engineering, traditional testing methods become insufficient for capturing the performance of self-optimizing systems. Koc emphasizes that as AI behavior becomes more fluid and context-dependent, evaluation frameworks must also become dynamic and adaptive. This presentation is particularly beneficial for AI engineers, researchers, and developers looking for more robust ways to measure and understand the performance of modern, agentic LLM applications.

queue.title: Data vs Hype: How Orgs Actually Win with AI - The Pragmatic Summit
queue.summary: Laura Tacho provides a pragmatic, data-driven analysis of how AI is currently impacting software development organizations, moving beyond the industry hype. She highlights key industry benchmarks, noting that while over 92% of developers use AI assistants, the average time savings remains steady at about four hours per week, even as the volume of AI-authored code continues to rise. The talk also reveals significant benefits in cutting onboarding time in half, though it warns that AI acts as an accelerator that can either reduce incidents or double them depending on an organization's existing structural health. This video is highly beneficial for CTOs, engineering leaders, and tech managers looking to understand the realistic outcomes and performance metrics of AI implementation in technical teams.

queue.title: CI/CD Is Dead, Agents Need Continuous Compute and Computers — Hugo Santos and Madison Faulkner
queue.summary: This video explores how Agentic Software—AI-driven code generation and management—is fundamentally changing traditional software development lifecycles. The speakers argue that traditional CI/CD pipelines, designed for the relatively slow and predictable pace of human developers, are being overwhelmed by the high volume and speed of AI agents. To address this, they propose a shift toward Continuous Compute, which uses an agent-aware orchestration layer to handle rapid code commits, automated validation, and complex merging processes. This presentation is particularly beneficial for software engineers, DevOps professionals, and AI researchers interested in modernizing infrastructure for an AI-integrated development environment.

queue.title: 39C3 - Agentic ProbLLMs: Exploiting AI Computer-Use and Coding Agents
queue.summary: This video features security researcher Johann Rehberger discussing the vulnerabilities of AI computer-use and coding agents, specifically focusing on prompt injection attacks. He demonstrates how these autonomous agents can be manipulated through indirect prompt injection to perform malicious actions, such as downloading and executing malware from a website. This talk is a must-watch for security professionals and AI developers, as it highlights the critical need for robust safety frameworks in the rapidly evolving landscape of agentic AI.

queue.title: I was laid off by Atlassian
queue.summary: The video features a former Atlassian employee reflecting on his eight-year tenure and the technical projects he built, specifically detailing his interview process and his first project involving an Open Service Broker for self-service load balancing. Using tools like Python, FastAPI, and AWS, he explains the architecture and evolution of the platform's infrastructure during his time there. This video is aimed at developers and engineers, offering both inspiration and technical insights into building scalable internal services and navigating a career in big tech. It would be particularly helpful for those interested in infrastructure engineering or anyone currently experiencing a similar career transition.

queue.title: Combine Skills and MCP to Close the Context Gap — Pedro Rodrigues, Supabase
queue.summary: This video features Pedro, an AI Tooling Engineer at Supabase, sharing insights and lessons learned from developing "skills" to help AI agents better understand and interact with the Supabase platform. He presents three core principles for teaching agents: describe intent rather than means to avoid documentation duplication, include critical information like security checklists directly in the main skill file to prevent it from being skipped, and provide opinionated guidance on optimal workflows. Additionally, the talk introduces an experimental feature where documentation is exposed over SSH, allowing agents to navigate it like a file system. This presentation is particularly valuable for AI engineers and developers building AI-integrated products who want to enhance the precision and efficiency of AI agents within their software ecosystems.

queue.title: Harnesses in AI: A Deep Dive — Tejas Kumar, IBM
queue.summary: Tejas Kumar explains the concept and critical role of "AI harnesses" in developing reliable AI agents at the AI Engineer Europe conference. He defines a harness as the supporting infrastructure—including tool registries, guardrails, and verification steps—that grounds a "black box" model in a stable, real-world environment. Using a practical demonstration, Kumar illustrates how a well-constructed harness can significantly improve the performance and reliability of AI agents, even when utilizing less powerful models. This talk is highly valuable for AI engineers and developers looking to build more robust and consistent AI-driven applications.

queue.title: Why Your AI UX Is Broken (and It's Not the Model's Fault) — Mike Christensen, Ably
queue.summary: This presentation by Mike Christensen from Ably explores why current AI user experiences are often fragile and how to improve them through better architecture. He argues that the standard model of direct HTTP streaming using Server-Sent Events (SSE) lacks resilience to disconnections and fails to provide a seamless experience across multiple devices or tabs. Christensen proposes the use of "durable sessions"—a stateful layer that decouples the agent from the client—along with bidirectional transports like WebSockets to enable live control and continuity. AI engineers and developers building production-grade AI chat or assistant applications would benefit from watching this to learn how to build more robust and interactive user interfaces.

queue.title: Cooking with Agents in VS Code — Liam Hampton, Microsoft
queue.summary: Liam Hampton explores how developers can utilize different types of AI agents within VS Code to streamline workflows and reduce cognitive load. He categorizes tools into local agents for interactive tasks, background agents for autonomous local tasks using Git worktrees, and cloud agents for remote long-running tasks. Through a practical demo, he showcases a simultaneous workflow using all three agent types to update, test, and document a Python application, highlighting VS Code as a unified entry point for shifting from writing raw code to orchestrating outcomes.

queue.title: Fast Models Need Slow Developers — Sarah Chieng, Cerebras
queue.summary: Addresses the rapid acceleration of AI code generation, highlighting how newer models like Codex Spark on Cerebras can generate over 1,200 tokens per second. This speedup is driven by full-stack optimizations including disaggregated inference, Mixture of Experts architectures, and KV cache reuse. To prevent fast models from compounding unverified technical debt, developers should use a tiered approach: larger slower models for planning, ultra-fast models for execution, and saving successful agent workflows as repeatable skills.

queue.title: Lobster Trap: OpenClaw in Containers from Local to K8s and Back — Sally Ann O'Malley, Red Hat
queue.summary: Sally Ann O'Malley from Red Hat discusses migrating a local OpenClaw AI agent configuration into a reproducible and secure production baseline using containerization. Key benefits of running AI workloads in containers include environment reproducibility, secrets isolation, and portability across infrastructures. Covers practical strategies for managing API keys with Podman and Kubernetes secrets, and lifting local containerized setups into Kubernetes clusters. Highlights a real-world example of how Nvidia utilizes OpenClaw on Kubernetes to streamline and scale model evaluation pipelines.

queue.title: Personalization in the Era of LLMs - Shivam Verma, Spotify
queue.summary: Shivam Verma from Spotify outlines the company's transition from traditional multi-stage recommendation pipelines to unified foundation models and sequential transformer-based architectures. Spotify generates dense user embeddings daily to represent taste profiles across music, podcasts, and audiobooks. By feeding sequential user history, context, and prompts into a transformer model, they map users and catalog items into a shared vector space. This enables interactive steerable personalization features like Prompted Playlists and the AI DJ, allowing users to refine recommendations using natural language.

queue.title: Rewiring the State — Eoin Mulgrew, No. 10 (Downing Street)
queue.summary: Outlines the UK government's efforts to address public sector productivity crisis through strategic AI adoption, led by the 10 Downing Street Data Science team (10DS). To overcome barriers like lower pay and bureaucracy that discourage top tech talent, 10DS established the No10 Innovation Fellowship — an insurgency model recruiting skilled outsiders at market-competitive rates with autonomy to drive rapid transformation. The fellowship operates through two pathways: forward-deploying engineers into policy teams for quick AI solutions, and partnering on long-term initiatives to resolve complex systemic backlogs.

queue.title: Scaling Agents on Kubernetes with acpx and ACP — Onur Solmaz, OpenClaw
queue.summary: Explores the integration of OpenClaw, an open-source agent framework, with the Agent Client Protocol (ACP) to standardize human-to-agent and agent-to-agent interactions. The protocol allows developers to run collaborative AI workflows — such as pull request triage and document generation — directly within communication platforms like Discord and Telegram. Introduces acpx, a CLI tool designed to streamline communication between different AI models. Highlights how adopting standardized protocols like ACP can reduce redundant development efforts and accelerate enterprise adoption of AI agents.

queue.title: Skill issue: Lessons from skilling up coding agents to use Langfuse - Marc Klingen, Clickhouse
queue.summary: Marc Klingen discusses bridging the gap between reliable structured workflows and autonomous agents by defining skills — formalized, reusable shortcuts that give agents optimized paths to achieve specific goals. To keep agents performing optimally, developers should trace agent runtimes to capture production signals, identify new use cases, and continuously refine existing skills. Standard coding agents often struggle with library integrations due to outdated pre-training data and hallucinated parameters. Langfuse resolved this by building a dedicated skill that structures up-to-date documentation, API specs, and CLI tools directly for the agent.

queue.title: The Missing Primitive for Agent Swarms — Lou Bichard, Ona
queue.summary: Lou Bichard discusses building a software factory by incrementally automating the software development lifecycle using autonomous AI agent swarms and fleets. Outlines key architectural patterns for scaling coding agents, highlighting why full VM isolation is superior to containers for ensuring security, performance, and noise isolation during execution. Demonstrates the Ona platform, showing how a parent agent coordinates parallel VM- or process-level sub-agents to complete complex development tasks and maintain workflow state.

queue.title: What Breaks When You Build AI Under Sovereignty Constraints - Bilge Yücel, deepset GmbH
queue.summary: Defines Sovereign AI as an organization's ability to design, deploy, and operate AI systems on its own terms, structured around four pillars: data, model, infrastructure, and operational sovereignty. Achieving AI sovereignty is a spectrum, and transitioning existing systems introduces major engineering hurdles such as migrating private data, managing self-hosted models, and dealing with hardware limitations. Orchestration frameworks like Haystack can help by offering consistent interfaces, explicit data flows, and extensibility to make AI systems more controllable and adaptable.

queue.title: Your Agent Is an Infinite Canvas — RL Nabors, Dressed for Space
queue.summary: Rachel Lee Nabors introduces the agentic web concept, exploring how AI agents and browsers can collaborate to transform content consumption. Details the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and compares STDIO and HTTP transports, explaining how HTTP allows developers to host MCP servers as scalable web services rather than local processes. Demonstrates by turning a personal web comic archive into an HTTP MCP server, enabling AI agents like Claude to query the database and retrieve structured data. Emphasizes leveraging existing standard web protocols to make websites easily accessible and interactive for AI agents.

queue.title: Your Coding Agent Should Do AI System Engineering — Ben Burtenshaw, Hugging Face
queue.summary: Advocates for using advanced coding agents to tackle complex AI systems and ML engineering tasks such as writing custom CUDA kernels and fine-tuning LLMs. Introduces Hugging Face's kernels repository, a platform for publishing and distributing optimized compute kernels to overcome GPU memory bottlenecks and accelerate model inference. Highlights the use of Skills — file-based contexts that guide agents through few-shot learning — alongside the upskill library for benchmarking agent performance. These frameworks enable AI engineers to optimize workloads closer to the hardware level and automate sophisticated model-training processes.

queue.title: Scaling the Next Paradigm of Heterogeneous Intelligence — Adrian Bertagnoli, Callosum
queue.summary: Adrian Bertagnoli, a founding engineer at Callosum, presents a case for transitioning from homogeneous AI systems to heterogeneous intelligence, which integrates diverse model architectures and specialized hardware. He explains that because real-world problems are complex and multi-faceted, utilizing a variety of specialized agent models on optimized hardware is mathematically proven to outperform single, dense models under resource constraints. He introduces heterogeneous recursion, demonstrating that routing sub-contexts of long-context tasks to optimal chips (such as Cerebras or Sambanova) can make processing up to 12 times cheaper and 5 times faster than standard models. This presentation is valuable for AI engineers, system architects, and researchers interested in the future of scaling and optimizing large language model workflows.

queue.title: Agentic Evaluations at Scale, For Everybody — Nicholas Kang & Michael Aaron, Google DeepMind
queue.summary: In this presentation from the AI Engineer Europe conference, Kaggle Product Manager Nicholas Kang discusses the challenges and potential solutions for agentic AI evaluation at scale. Kang argues that current AI evaluations are broken because they are scattered, lack transparency, and are primarily created by a narrow group of researchers rather than diverse domain experts. To address this, he introduces Kaggle's initiatives, such as collaborative hackathons and standardized agent exams, to make AI benchmarks more accessible and robust. This video is highly beneficial for AI developers, researchers, and data scientists interested in building more reliable and comprehensive evaluation methods for AI models.

queue.title: Bounded Autonomy: Between Free Will and Determinism — Angus J. McLean, Oliver
queue.summary: This video features Angus McLean, AI Director at Oliver, discussing the practical applications and design challenges of using AI agents for speed and scale in the advertising industry. He advises developers to slow down amidst rapid AI advancements, emphasizing that the fundamental limitations of large language models—such as data inefficiency and a lack of true understanding—remain unchanged. McLean argues that instead of chasing massive context windows, developers should intentionally constrain context to reduce noise, as limitations can actually foster greater creativity. This talk is highly beneficial for both beginners and experts actively experimenting with AI agents who are looking for a practical and grounded perspective on agent design.

queue.title: Stop babysitting your agents... — Brandon Walsenuk, Unblocked
queue.summary: This presentation focuses on the necessity of building robust context engines to make AI agents more autonomous, eliminating the need for developers to constantly monitor and correct them. The speaker argues that the primary bottleneck for current AI agents is not intelligence, but a lack of contextual understanding, which cannot be solved merely through naive RAG, simple tool access, or larger context windows. Instead, an effective context engine must integrate static, runtime, and social graph data to resolve conflicting information, respect governance, and deliver precise, token-optimized context. This video is highly beneficial for software engineers, AI developers, and engineering leaders looking to successfully implement and scale AI agents within their workflows.

queue.title: What the Best Agents Share — Mardu Swanepoel, Flinn AI
queue.summary: This video presents a talk by Mardu Swanepoel on how to design more effective AI agents by analyzing and adopting design patterns from the industry's best examples. He outlines four key patterns—focused modes, transparent execution, personalization, and reversibility—that help improve agent output, foster user trust, and lower the risks associated with AI mistakes. AI developers and engineers will benefit from watching this to learn how to transition their agent designs from simple delegation tools to collaborative, user-centric systems.

queue.title: Most Enterprise Agentic Projects Are Doomed, Here's Why — Jess Grogan-Avignon & Jack Wang, Accenture
queue.summary: This video features a presentation by Jess and Jack at AI Engineer Europe on why most enterprise AI agentic projects are doomed to fail due to a clash between traditional human-paced structures and rapid machine-speed advancements. The speakers outline key enterprise tensions, arguing that organizations are bottlenecked by legacy approval infrastructures and that traditional, certainty-driven business cases are ill-suited for the emergent nature of AI. This talk is highly valuable for enterprise leaders, AI engineers, and financial decision-makers looking to understand how to restructure their organizations and adopt a venture-capital mindset to successfully scale AI initiatives.

queue.title: Así Te EXPLOTA el Capitalismo Digital (y NO Te Das Cuenta)
queue.summary: This video features an interview with researcher Mariano Zukerfeld, who discusses the evolution of digital capitalism, specifically focusing on the transition from the network phase to the platform phase. He argues that modern digital platforms and artificial intelligence exploit human knowledge, user data, and social connections under the guise of free services, effectively commodifying human relationships. Additionally, Zukerfeld highlights the historical expansion of intellectual property rights as a mechanism of modern economic dominance. This video would be highly beneficial for students, sociologists, economists, and anyone interested in the socio-economic impacts of digital technology and AI.

queue.title: There Is No AI, Really (It’s Just People), with Jaron Lanier
queue.summary: This video features an interview between astrophysicist Neil deGrasse Tyson and computer scientist Jaron Lanier, the pioneer who coined the term 'virtual reality.' Lanier discusses his original conception of VR as a contrast medium to help people appreciate physical reality, rather than an escapist alternative, and addresses the issue of VR-induced motion sickness, noting its disproportionate effect on non-white women. He also reflects on his unique role as an internal critic at Microsoft and the massive financial investments made in the VR industry. Tech enthusiasts, futurists, and anyone interested in the philosophical and social implications of digital technology will find this video highly engaging and informative.

queue.title: Why your agents need decision traces, not just documents — Zach Blumenfeld, Neo4j
queue.summary: In this presentation, Zach from Neo4j explains how context graphs elevate AI agents beyond simple query-answering to make robust, institutionally grounded decisions. By integrating past decision traces, precedents, and hybrid search (combining text and graph embeddings), context graphs enable agents to analyze complex scenarios and provide explanation-backed recommendations. Zach also introduces create-context-graph, a new open-source CLI tool that allows developers to spin up a full-stack context graph agent in under five minutes. This video is highly beneficial for AI engineers and developers seeking to build more accurate, reliable, and decision-capable AI systems.

queue.title: Yann LeCun's $1B Bet Against LLMs [Part 2]
queue.summary: Compares Vision Language Action (VLA) models for robotics with JEPA, critiquing VLAs' reliance on behavioral cloning and lack of explicit planning. Shows how JEPA-based approaches learn world models — self-supervised simulations — to plan action sequences in embedding space, allowing robots to understand physics and plan complex tasks more efficiently than generative models.

queue.title: Yann LeCun's $1B Bet Against LLMs
queue.summary: Explores Yann LeCun's Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) as an alternative to generative models like LLMs. Argues JEPA can learn powerful world representations without generating data, mimicking how humans learn. Positions JEPA-based world models as a path to more efficient, human-like machine intelligence.

queue.title: Zig 2026: No-AI Policy, $670K Foundation, Left GitHub & Why Zig Isn’t 1.0 - Andrew Kelley Explains
queue.summary: In this interview, Andrew Kelley, the creator of the Zig programming language, discusses his journey of developing Zig after experiencing frustrations with languages like C++, Rust, Go, and JavaScript while building a digital audio workstation. He explains that Zig was built to give developers full control over the computer, allowing for optimal performance and memory usage without compromising the user experience. The video also highlights real-world use cases of Zig, including applications like Ghosty, TigerBeetle, Bun, and Uber's development toolchain. This video is highly beneficial for software engineers, systems programmers, and anyone interested in the evolution of modern programming languages.

queue.title: Spec-Driven Testing for Agents With A Brain the Size of A Planet — Steven Willmott, SafeIntelligence
queue.summary: In this presentation, Steven Willmott, CEO of Safe Intelligence, introduces "spec-driven validation" as a more comprehensive framework for testing and evaluating AI agents compared to simple datasets. He argues that deploying larger, "smarter" AI models actually increases security vulnerabilities and costs, necessitating a testing approach focused on balancing capability with safety. Willmott outlines how incorporating rules, domain knowledge, ontologies, and user rights into an agent's specification allows for more rigorous security and robustness testing under stress. This video is highly beneficial for AI developers, engineers, and enterprises looking to deploy secure and reliable AI agents in production environments.

queue.title: 20 days of compute vs 7 hours: rethinking what state-of-the-art means — Bertrand Charpentier, Pruna
queue.summary: Explores the challenge of determining which AI model is truly 'state-of-the-art' for a given application. Argues that common evaluation practices — relying on generic public leaderboards or subjective manual inspections — are deeply flawed because rankings vary wildly depending on the specific task, and human reviewers are highly biased toward their own preferences. No single model dominates every scenario, meaning developers must evaluate options using statistically significant data under conditions that closely mirror their final use case. Beneficial for AI engineers, product managers, and researchers selecting and deploying AI models for specific applications.

queue.title: Can LLMs generate Enterprise Quality Code? — Prasenjit Sarkar, Sonar
queue.summary: This video discusses Sonar's evaluation of whether LLMs can generate enterprise-quality code, highlighting that standard benchmarks fail to measure critical aspects like code maintainability, complexity, and security. Sonar developed an evaluation framework analyzing over 50 LLMs against 4,400+ Java assignments, revealing that high-performing models often produce highly verbose, buggy, and insecure code. Software developers, AI engineers, and tech architects would benefit from watching this to understand the risks and realities of AI coding assistants in enterprise environments.

queue.title: How I deleted 95% of my agent skills and got better results — Nick Nisi, WorkOS
queue.summary: Presents a talk on building and shipping reliable AI systems using specialized, multi-agent workflows controlled by state machines. Explains how to prevent AI agents from skipping steps by implementing strict verification loops and limiting context with human-curated 'gotcha' lists rather than massive auto-generated guides. Demonstrates through rigorous evaluation that overloading agents with unnecessary context can paradoxically degrade performance from 97% down to 77%. Valuable for AI engineers and software developers deploying coding agents and developer-focused AI tools into production.

queue.title: How Lovable self-improves every hour — Benjamin Verbeek, Lovable
queue.summary: Discusses how Lovable's AI-powered platform continuously self-improves to make software creation accessible to people who cannot code. Explains how the company identifies when users get stuck on technical issues and uses a system called 'Lovable Stack Overflow' to capture, cluster, and test solutions. By continuously updating this knowledge base, the platform injects fixes into future user sessions, ensuring mistakes only happen once. Beneficial for AI engineers, developers, and product designers interested in continuous learning at scale and building intuitive AI tools.

queue.title: How to talk to statues — Joe Reeve, ElevenLabs
queue.summary: Introduces a viral AI-powered app built in two hours that allows users to have voice conversations with museum statues. Explains how the app integrates OpenAI's multimodal model for identification and research with ElevenLabs' APIs to generate custom voices and interactive agents in under 30 seconds. Explores the concept of 'vibe coding' (rapid prototyping with AI) and raises philosophical questions about designing voices for inanimate objects as voice interfaces become more common. Beneficial for AI developers, creators, and those interested in the future of voice technology and rapid app development.

queue.title: Task Fidelity Scaling Laws — Kobie Crawdord, Snorkel
queue.summary: Discusses Task Fidelity Scaling Laws and how the quality of training tasks affects AI model performance. Argues that high-quality, curated tasks (achievable, non-trivial, functionally correct, and reliable) provide more complex, useful challenges with cleaner failure modes compared to low-quality, noisy tasks. Through RL training experiments, fine-tuning on high-quality tasks leads to a substantial 6.2 percentage point performance improvement, compared to a mere 1.1 percentage point gain from low-quality tasks. Beneficial for AI engineers, ML researchers, and data scientists optimizing fine-tuning for agentic AI models.

queue.title: What if the network was the sandbox? — Remy Guercio, Tailscale
queue.summary: Explores the concept of utilizing the network layer as a secure sandbox for AI agents. Argues that by integrating identity and permissions (AuthN and AuthZ) directly into network connections using WireGuard, organizations can eliminate the need to store sensitive API keys within sandbox environments. Demonstrates Aperture, an AI gateway that leverages network identity to control, monitor, and audit LLM requests based on specific user or runner tags. Beneficial for AI security engineers, cloud architects, and developers looking for robust, keyless methods to govern and secure AI infrastructure.

queue.title: What Lies Beneath the API — Benjamin Cowen, Modal
queue.summary: Explores the strategic transition from utilizing frontier APIs to fine-tuning custom AI models as a product matures and specializes. Argues that while frontier APIs offer a rapid starting point, they lack customization, prompting developers to eventually seek the performance and cost benefits of fine-tuning. Presents serverless compute platforms as a modern middle ground, bridging the gap by providing full algorithmic control and rapid iteration without the overhead of managing Kubernetes clusters. Beneficial for AI engineers, ML developers, and startup founders optimizing their AI product development lifecycle.

archive.title: Building an Electric Wind-Up Plane
archive.summary: Documents the design and construction of a 21st-century wind-up plane, replacing rubber bands with supercapacitors and a hand-crank generator. Showcases innovative 3D printing directly onto tissue paper for lightweight wing surfaces, and computational simulations for flight stability. Hobbyists, makers, and engineering enthusiasts will benefit from this detailed look at modernizing classic toy technology.

archive.title: Building pi in a World of Slop — Mario Zechner
archive.summary: Mario Zechner introduces pi, a minimal and highly extensible coding agent designed to replace bloated alternatives. He argues that current coding agents suffer from slop — unnecessary features and poor context management — and proposes that agents should be malleable and self-modifying. Using a small token footprint and TypeScript-based extensions, pi allows developers to adapt the tool to their specific workflows. Ideal for AI engineers and developers seeking a more flexible, developer-centric approach to AI-assisted coding.

archive.title: Season 21, Episode 1 - 'Cube is good.' | Full Episode
archive.summary: Taskmaster S21E01 premiere where contestants present humorous deterrents to protect their property, judged by Greg Davies for creativity and comedic value. A recurring secret task requires Joel Dommett to keep a specially decorated egg intact throughout the series. Fans of comedy panel shows and British humor will enjoy this.

archive.title: Why was I invited to Beast Studios?
archive.summary: Dan Olson (Folding Ideas) chronicles his visit to MrBeast's studios for an exclusive tour and preview of Beast Games Season 2. He breaks down the trip as a wine and dine PR move, providing an itemized cost analysis of the hospitality. Insightful for viewers interested in corporate PR, YouTube culture, and the business behind large-scale creators.

archive.title: We finally watched Tip2Tip
archive.summary: Ludwig and Michael react to the first episode of their series 'Tip to Tip China,' which documents their 4,000-kilometer motorcycle journey across the country. They humorously critique their own attempts at speaking Mandarin, specifically laughing at Ludwig's repeated linguistic errors and his reliance on ChatGPT for local slang. The reaction highlights their awkward interactions with locals and the challenges of navigating a foreign country with limited language skills.

archive.title: Making human kibble (to change the way we eat forever)
archive.summary: The video explores the creation of 'human kibble,' a concept designed to save the time people typically spend preparing and eating food. The creator uses a high-powered blender to homogenize popular fast foods, including McDonald's Big Macs, Domino's pepperoni pizza, and KFC chicken meals, with distilled water to create a smooth, drinkable consistency. After blending each meal and confirming they still retain their original flavors through taste tests, the resulting mixtures are spread onto metal trays, presumably to be freeze-dried into pellets in the next stage of the process.

archive.title: #191 María O'Donnell en Cartografías
archive.summary: The video features an interview with Maria O'Donnell about her book, 'Montoneros: una historia visual,' which provides a graphical and accessible account of the Argentine armed political organization from the 1970s. O'Donnell explains how the book aims to educate younger generations on the group's complex history, including its rise during the proscription of Peronism and the impact of pivotal events like the assassination of General Aramburu.

archive.title: A love letter to Pi | Lucas Meijer
archive.summary: This video features a presentation by Lucas Meijer, a former Unity developer, on the practical application of AI coding agents in software development. Meijer shares several strategies, such as using specific AI models like Codex, embracing HTML as an output format for agent reports, and making codebases agent-friendly by reducing build warnings and providing clear documentation. He emphasizes analyzing previous agent sessions to identify friction points and advises defining evaluation criteria before tasking an agent. Highly relevant for programmers looking to integrate AI agents into their workflows effectively.

archive.title: How Building with AI Can Double the Throughput of Your Engineering Team — Brian Scanlan, Intercom
archive.summary: This video features Brian, a Senior Principal Engineer at Intercom, discussing how the company leveraged AI to double the throughput of their engineering teams. He emphasizes the importance of organizational change, such as updating job descriptions and rewarding AI adoption, alongside a standardized platform strategy using tools like Claude Code. By integrating company-specific engineering guidance and fostering a continuous improvement flywheel, Intercom successfully transformed into an AI-first company. This talk is highly beneficial for engineering leaders, AI developers, and anyone interested in the practical application of AI to enhance corporate productivity.

archive.title: How Does Google Maps Actually Work?
archive.summary: Explores pathfinding algorithms from Dijkstra's 1956 shortest-path to modern mapping systems. Covers A*, bidirectional search, and customizable contraction hierarchies — how road networks are preprocessed for near-instant routing. Shows how elegant math solves a computationally daunting problem in milliseconds.

archive.title: Inside the World's Smartest Robot Brain [VLA]
archive.summary: This video details the rapid evolution of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, which utilize large language models to serve as unified "brains" for physical robots. It traces the progression of these systems from Google's early iterations like SayCan, Palm-E, and RT-2—highlighting a landmark demonstration where a robot successfully moved a coke can to a picture of Taylor Swift—to Physical Intelligence's highly efficient Pi-0 model performing dexterous tasks like folding laundry and uncapping pens. A key takeaway is that VLAs can bridge internet-scale visual and textual knowledge with robotic actuators, enabling impressive zero-shot generalization to novel objects and environments. Finally, the video explains how Pi-0 integrates a flow-matching "action expert" to achieve high performance locally on consumer-grade hardware, while noting that alternative paradigms like world models are also emerging.

archive.title: They Teach AI Music at Music School Now...
archive.summary: Explores Berklee College's push for generative AI songwriting courses and the backlash over connections between school leadership and AI music companies like Suno. Argues Berklee is out of touch with students and the industry, advocating for human-centered musical education over controversial AI reliance.
